1: A direct allusion: 'For everything there is a season, and a time for every matter under heaven.' The time-for-everything principle — creation ordered by divinely appointed seasons. Galatians 4:4: 'But when the fullness of time had come [to pleroma tou chronou], God sent forth his Son, born of woman, born under the law.' The 'fullness of time' is the decisive appointed-time that governs all other appointed times: there was a time for everything, and when the appointed time arrived, the Son entered the sequence. The entire temporal ordering Qohelet surveys had been moving toward this moment. Ecclesiastes 3:1 is the theological premise; Galatians 4:4 is its fulfillment-statement.
2: A type: 'a time to be born, and a time to die; a time to plant, and a time to pluck up what is planted.' The birth-and-death pairing within the created order. John 12:23-24: 'The hour has come for the Son of Man to be glorified. Truly, truly, I say to you, unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit.' Christ's birth-and-death follows the Qohelet time-sequence, but with a crucial addition: the dying produces fruit rather than vanity. The 'time to die' that Qohelet observes as simply the rhythm of creation becomes in Christ the moment of maximum fruitfulness — the death that is actually a planting.
3: A shadow: 'a time to kill, and a time to heal; a time to break down, and a time to build up.' The healing-time in the Qohelet sequence. Luke 4:18-19: 'He has sent me to proclaim liberty to the captives and recovering of sight to the blind, to set at liberty those who are oppressed, to proclaim the year of the Lord's favor.' Christ's announcement of his ministry is entirely in the register of healing and building up — the two positive items in Qohelet's pairing. The accumulated broken-down, killed, wounded condition of humanity in the fall-season is addressed in the healing ministry of Christ, who enters the 'time to heal' and the 'time to build up.'
4: A shadow: 'a time to weep, and a time to laugh; a time to mourn, and a time to dance.' The mourning-to-dancing sequence that creation embeds in time. John 11:35: 'Jesus wept' — he entered the mourning-time with those who mourned Lazarus, not bypassing grief but entering it. John 20:20: 'the disciples were glad when they saw the Lord' — the resurrection produces the dancing-time. The Qohelet time-sequence of mourning and dancing is lived out by Christ: he wept at the tomb, entered the death himself, and on the third day the disciples saw him and rejoiced. The time-pattern is not meaningless cycling; in Christ it has a one-directional resolution: mourning to joy.
5: A shadow: 'a time to cast away stones, and a time to gather stones together; a time to embrace, and a time to refrain from embracing.' The refraining-from-embrace is the unusual item. John 20:17: 'Do not cling to me, for I have not yet ascended to the Father; but go to my brothers and say to them, 'I am ascending to my Father and your Father.'' The risen Christ instructing Mary not to cling is the 'time to refrain from embracing' — not a withdrawal of love but the transition to a new mode of presence. The embrace-and-refrain rhythm in Qohelet finds its fulfillment in the resurrection appearances that are preparing the disciples for the Spirit's mode of Christ's presence.
6: A shadow: 'a time to seek, and a time to lose; a time to keep, and a time to cast away.' The seeking-time. Luke 15 (the three parables of the lost): the shepherd seeking the lost sheep, the woman seeking the lost coin, the father running to receive the returning son. Christ as the one who enters the 'time to seek' with maximum intentionality — 'the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost' (Luke 19:10). The Qohelet time-for-seeking is the temporal structure within which the seeking God operates; Jesus embodies that divine seeking in his ministry.
7: A shadow: 'a time to tear, and a time to sew; a time to keep silence, and a time to speak.' The tearing-and-sewing. Mark 15:38: 'the curtain of the temple was torn in two, from top to bottom.' The tearing of the temple curtain at Christ's death is the definitive 'time to tear' — the barrier between God and humanity torn open. The silence is also Christological: Matthew 26:63; Ecclesiastes 27:12-14 — Jesus remained silent before his accusers, fulfilling Isaiah 53:7: 'he opened not his mouth.' The time-for-silence and the time-for-speech are both embodied in Christ's passion.
8: A direct connection: 'a time to love, and a time to hate; a time for war, and a time for peace.' The love-time. John 15:13: 'Greater love has no one than this, that someone lay down his life for his friends.' Christ's death is the definitive 'time to love' — the moment in the temporal sequence when the love of God was maximally expressed. Romans 5:8: 'God shows his love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.' The time-for-love that Qohelet includes in the creation's temporal rhythm is fulfilled in the event of the cross — the appointed time when love achieved its maximum expression.
9: A theme: 'What gain has the worker from his toil?' The yitron-question again. 1 Corinthians 15:58: 'your labor in the Lord is not in vain.' The answer to the toil-question appears twice in Ecclesiastes (1:3 and Ecclesiastes 3:9) and each time Paul's answer is the same: the resurrection transforms the zero-gain equation. The question recurs because it is the book's central question; its answer requires the resurrection to be given.
10: A shadow: 'I have seen the business that God has given to the children of man to be busy with.' The 'business God has given' — the creational task given to humanity. John 9:4: 'We must work the works of him who sent me while it is day; night is coming, when no one can work.' Christ takes the 'business God has given' language and applies it to his own mission: he is the one who does the Father's work while the time remains. The Qohelet phrase 'business God has given to the children of man' becomes in Christ 'the works of him who sent me' — the task is divinely assigned and has its own appointed time.
11: A direct connection: 'He has made everything beautiful in its time. He has also put eternity into man's heart, yet so that he cannot find out what God has done from the beginning to the end.' The eternity-in-the-heart — the deepest anthropological observation in the book. John 14:6: 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.' The eternity that God has placed in the human heart as an unfillable longing is answered by Christ, who is himself eternal life (1 John 5:20: 'This is the true God and eternal life'). What Qohelet observes as the human capacity for eternity that nothing earthly can fill, Christ declares himself to be the content of. Colossians 1:27: 'Christ in you, the hope of glory.'
12: A theme: 'I perceived that there is nothing better for them than to be joyful and to do good as long as they live.' The joy-and-good-works prescription. John 15:11: 'These things I have spoken to you, that my joy may be in you, and that your joy may be full.' Christ promises not merely joy as an abstract good but his own joy — the joy that the eternal Son has in the Father — transplanted into the believer. The Qohelet prescription for joy (be joyful; do good) is the wisdom-tradition form of what Christ calls his disciples to; the source and quality of the joy is transformed in Christ from a choice to a gift.
13: A direct allusion: 'also that everyone should eat and drink and take pleasure in his toil — this is God's gift.' The eat-and-drink-and-enjoy formula as God's gift. John 6:53-56: 'For my flesh is true food, and my blood is true drink... Whoever feeds on my flesh and drinks my blood abides in me, and I in him.' The Eucharist is the transformation of the Qohelet eat-and-drink pattern from its natural form into its sacramental fulfillment. The 'God's gift' of eating and drinking in Ecclesiastes becomes the gift of Christ's body and blood — the gift of God in its most concentrated form. 'This is God's gift' and 'this is my body given for you' are linked across the canon.
14: A direct connection: 'I perceived that whatever God does endures forever; nothing can be added to it, nor anything taken from it. God has done it, so that people fear before him.' The permanence of God's work — whatever he does stands. Hebrews 13:8: 'Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever.' John 1:1: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.' Christ is the eternal one who does permanent work; what Qohelet observes as the general principle (God's work endures), Christ embodies personally. The resurrection is the irreversible act of God — the permanent work that 'nothing can be added to, nor anything taken from.'
15: A direct connection: 'That which is, already has been; that which is to be, already has been; and God seeks out what has been driven away.' 'God seeks out what has been driven away' is the most explicit statement in the wisdom literature of the seeking God. Luke 15:4-7 (the lost sheep): 'What man of you, having a hundred sheep, if he has lost one of them, does not leave the ninety-nine in the open country, and go after the lost one until he finds it?' Luke 15:11-32 (the prodigal son): the father runs to meet the returning son. Christ's three lost-parables in Luke 15 are the definitive 'God seeks out what has been driven away' — they are the narrative expansion of Ecclesiastes 3:15b. The God who seeks the driven-away becomes the shepherd, the woman, and the father.
16: A shadow: 'Moreover, I saw under the sun that in the place of justice, even there was wickedness, and in the place of righteousness, even there was wickedness.' Justice corrupted at its very seat — the perversion of the judicial system. John 18:28-19:16: the trial of Jesus is the supreme instance of wickedness in the place of justice — the most unjust trial in history, conducted against the just one. The verdict of the Roman governor ('I find no guilt in him,' John 18:38; Ecclesiastes 19:4, 6) was publicly stated three times; then the unjust sentence was carried out. Qohelet's observation about wickedness in the place of justice is maximally exemplified in the passion narrative.
17: A direct connection: 'I said in my heart, God will judge the righteous and the wicked, for there is a time for every matter and for every work.' God as the ultimate judge who corrects earthly injustice. John 5:22, 27: 'The Father judges no one, but has given all judgment to the Son... And he has given him authority to execute judgment, because he is the Son of Man.' Christ is the appointed judge whom Qohelet points to when he says 'God will judge.' Acts 17:31: 'he has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed.' The judge Qohelet anticipates is identified in the NT as the risen Christ.
18: A shadow: 'I said in my heart with regard to the children of man that God is testing them that they may see that they themselves are but beasts.' The beast-comparison — humans tested to see their own animal nature. Hebrews 2:14-15: 'Since therefore the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise partook of the same things, that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil, and deliver all those who through fear of death were subject to lifelong slavery.' Christ entered the full creaturely limitation — including mortality — not because he was tested and found wanting, but voluntarily, to deliver those who were subject to it. He became what we are in order to make us what he is.
19: A type: 'For what happens to the children of man and what happens to the beasts is the same; as one dies, so dies the other. They all have the same breath, and man has no advantage over the beasts, for all is vanity.' The same-death-as-beasts observation — human mortality as not qualitatively different from animal death. Hebrews 2:14-17: Christ shared in flesh and blood 'that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death.' The 'same death as beasts' is the condition Christ entered — fully, without qualification — in order to break it. By dying as a man (sharing the fate Qohelet describes), he made death the site of victory rather than the final word.
20: A type: 'All go to one place. All are from the dust, and to dust all return.' Dust to dust — the common human fate. John 12:24: 'Truly, truly, I say to you, unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit.' Christ submitted to the dust-return explicitly: he was buried, descended into the earth as the grain of wheat. But the grain of wheat that falls into the earth does not remain dust — it rises as a plant bearing many grains. The dust-return that Qohelet sees as the terminus becomes in Christ the planting that precedes the harvest.
21: A direct connection: 'Who knows whether the spirit of man goes upward and the spirit of the beast goes downward to the earth?' Qohelet's open question about the human spirit's post-death destination. John 3:13: 'No one has ascended into heaven except he who descended from heaven, the Son of Man.' Acts 1:9-11: Christ's bodily ascension is the definitive answer to Qohelet's 'who knows whether the spirit goes upward.' The answer: the Son of Man has ascended, and his ascension is the pledge of the believer's resurrection and ascension (Ephesians 2:6: God 'raised us up with him and seated us with him in the heavenly places'). The question of 3:21 is answered by the event of Acts 1.
22: A shadow: 'So I saw that there is nothing better than that a man should rejoice in his work, for that is his lot. Who can bring him to see what will be after him?' The inability to see what comes after — the veil over the future. John 14:2-3: 'In my Father's house are many rooms. If it were not so, would I have told you that I go to prepare a place for you? And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again and will take you to myself, that where I am you may be also.' Christ answers Qohelet's 'who can bring him to see what will be' by providing exactly that: a revelation of what is prepared beyond death, and a promise to come again and bring his own to that place. The veil is lifted.
